<summary>
  <request>Research industry best practices for handling Telegram HTML message truncation in bot systems</request>
  <investigated>Analyzed the core problem in the Telegram bridge system where message truncation is based on Markdown length (3900 chars) but Telegram's 4096 limit applies to the final HTML output. Examined how Markdown-to-HTML conversion causes variable expansion rates: bold text expands slightly, code blocks expand 3x, blockquotes expand significantly, and HTML escaping adds overhead.</investigated>
  <learned>The current approach of truncating at a fixed Markdown length (TG_MAX = 3900) is flawed because it doesn't account for HTML expansion. This causes two problems: (1) markdown-heavy content may still exceed Telegram's 4096 HTML limit after conversion, and (2) plain text messages waste available space. The truncation also risks cutting mid-syntax (inside code blocks or markdown constructs), which breaks rendering.</learned>
  <completed>Identified four potential optimization strategies: (1) post-HTML truncation with HTML-safe cutting, (2) dynamic HTML length estimation based on markdown content density, (3) smarter cut-point selection to avoid breaking markdown syntax, and (4) unifying truncation logic between streaming and final render paths.   <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Framework-Specific HTML Splitting Solutions and NPM Packages Identified</title>
  <subtitle>Found GramIO Split Plugin for entity-preserving message splitting and mature NPM packages for HTML truncation</subtitle>

    <fact>npm package 'truncate-html' is actively maintained with custom node strategies and ellipsis configuration</fact>
    <fact>npm package 'html-truncate-ts' offers TypeScript support with flexible truncation options</fact>
    <fact>Telegram's 4096 limit applies to HTML source string (text parameter), not rendered text</fact>
    <fact>Telegram API supports only named entities < > & " but all numerical HTML entities work</fact>
    <fact>Streaming LLM bots use progressive editMessage calls (throttled to avoid rate limits) with final split into multiple messages if exceeding 4096</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Additional research uncovered framework-specific and package-based solutions for HTML message handling. GramIO's Split Plugin provides sophisticated entity-aware splitting that recalculates HTML entity offsets and lengths when messages are split across boundaries. Critically, Telegram's 4096 character limit applies to the HTML source string sent via the text parameter, not the rendered output, which simplifies measurement. Streaming LLM bots like ChatGPT clones use a pattern of progressive editMessage calls (throttled every 500ms or ~50 tokens) followed by splitting into multiple sequential messages if the final response exceeds 4096 characters. This confirms the industry standard is message splitting rather than truncation.</narrative>
